en.extract:
The red-chested cuckoo (Cuculus solitarius) is a species of cuckoo in the family Cuculidae. It is a medium-sized bird found in Africa, south of the Sahara. In Afrikaans, it is known as "Piet-my-vrou", after its call.


Description
The red-chested cuckoo is a medium-size cuckoo about 31 cm (12.2 in) in length. The male has slate-grey upper parts, pale grey throat and sides of head and dark grey tail tipped with white. The breast is rufous or cinnamon, often with barring, and the belly is creamy-white or pale buff. The female is similar but the colour of the breast is duller and with variable amounts of barring.


Distribution and habitat
It is found in Angola, Benin, Botswana, Burundi, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ivory Coast, Equatorial Guinea, Eswatini, Ethiopia, Gabon, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Kenya, Lesotho, Liberia, Malawi, Mali, Mozambique, Namibia, Nigeria, Rwanda, Senegal, Sierra Leone, Somalia, South Africa, Sudan, Tanzania, Togo, Uganda, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. In Southern Africa it is a common breeding migrant, found throughout the area except for the drier west. The preferred habitats for the red-chested cuckoo are woodlands. The red-chested cuckoo is normally seen by itself rather than in the company of birds of the same species.


Behavior
It is usually solitary and highly vocal and lives on forests and plantations. It eats insects including hairy caterpillars, spiders, centipedes, millipedes, slugs, snails, small vertebrates and berries.
The red-chested cuckoo takes on more than a single mate (it is polygamous). Like other cuckoos, the red-chested cuckoo lays its eggs in other birds' nests, leaving the parasitized birds to care for the cuckoo chicks, which they do, believing it is their own offspring (brood parasitism). About fifteen different species of small bird are victimized, but the most common hosts are the Cape robin-chat (Cossypha caffra), the Cape wagtail (Motacilla capensis) and the white-throated robin-chat (Cossypha humeralis). The surrogate family then raise the chick. The bird lays eggs which are brown in colour and number between 20 eggs per season in different nests.


References


External links
Red-chested cuckoo—Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

The '''red-chested cuckoo''' ('''''Cuculus solitarius''''') is a species of [[cuckoo]] in the family [[Cuculidae]]. It is a medium-sized bird found in Africa, [[Sub-Saharan Africa|south of the Sahara]]. In [[Afrikaans]], it is known as "Piet-my-vrou", after its call.<ref>{{cite book|last=Sinclair |first=Ian |title=Voëls van Suider-Afrika |date=31 July 1995 |publisher=Struik |isbn=1-86825-197-7}}</ref>

==Description==
The red-chested cuckoo is a medium-size cuckoo about {{convert|31|cm|in|1|abbr=on}} in length. The male has slate-grey upper parts, pale grey throat and sides of head and dark grey tail tipped with white. The breast is rufous or cinnamon, often with barring, and the belly is creamy-white or pale buff. The female is similar but the colour of the breast is duller and with variable amounts of barring.<ref name="Erritz">{{cite book|author1=Erritzøe, Johannes|author2=Mann, Clive F.|author3=Brammer, Frederik |author4=Fuller, Richard A.|title=Cuckoos of the World |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=avxBNNeRhCYC&pg=PA451 |year=2012 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4081-4267-7 |pages=451–453}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
It is found in [[Angola]], [[Benin]], [[Botswana]], [[Burundi]], [[Cameroon]], [[Central African Republic]], [[Chad]], [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], [[Ivory Coast]], [[Equatorial Guinea]], [[Eswatini]], [[Ethiopia]], [[Gabon]], [[Gambia]], [[Ghana]], [[Guinea]], [[Guinea-Bissau]], [[Kenya]], [[Lesotho]], [[Liberia]], [[Malawi]], [[Mali]], [[Mozambique]], [[Namibia]], [[Nigeria]], [[Rwanda]], [[Senegal]], [[Sierra Leone]], [[Somalia]], [[South Africa]], [[Sudan]], [[Tanzania]], [[Togo]], [[Uganda]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> In Southern Africa it is a common breeding migrant, found throughout the area except for the drier west. The preferred habitats for the red-chested cuckoo are woodlands. The red-chested cuckoo is normally seen by itself rather than in the company of birds of the same species.<ref name="Erritz" />

<gallery widths="200" heights="200">
File:Red-chested Cuckoo (Cuculus solitarius) in tree crop.jpg|alt=Male at Walter Sisulu Botanical Garden, South Africa|left|Male at [[Walter Sisulu National Botanical Garden|Walter Sisulu Botanical Garden]], South Africa
File:Red-chested_cuckoo_(Cuculus_solitarius)_female.jpg|A female in [[KwaZulu-Natal]], [[South Africa]]
File:Piet-my-vrou & cape robin.jpg|A juvenile bird being fed by a [[Cape robin-chat]] host
File:Cuculus solitarius, onvolw in Mkhuze-wildreservaat, Birding Weto, a.jpg|An immature bird in fresh plumage, South Africa
</gallery>

==Behavior==
It is usually solitary and highly vocal and lives on forests and plantations. It eats insects including hairy caterpillars, [[Spider|spiders]], [[centipede]]s, [[millipede]]s, [[Slug|slugs]], [[Snail|snails]], small vertebrates and berries.<ref name="Erritz" />

The red-chested cuckoo takes on more than a single mate (it is [[Animal sexual behaviour#Polygamy|polygamous]]). Like other cuckoos, the red-chested cuckoo lays its eggs in other birds' nests, leaving the parasitized birds to care for the cuckoo chicks, which they do, believing it is their own offspring ([[Brood parasite|brood parasitism]]). About fifteen different species of small bird are victimized, but the most common hosts are the [[Cape robin-chat]] (''Cossypha caffra''), the [[Cape wagtail]] (''Motacilla capensis'') and the [[white-throated robin-chat]] (''Cossypha humeralis'').<ref name="Izico">{{cite web |url=http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/cuculidae/cuculus_solitarius.htm |title=''Cuculus solitarius'' (Red-chested cuckoo) |work=Biodiversity Explorer |publisher=Izico |access-date=2014-06-10 |archive-date=2015-09-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923190148/http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/cuculidae/cuculus_solitarius.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> The surrogate family then raise the chick. The bird lays eggs which are brown in colour and number between 20 eggs per season in different nests.<ref name="Erritz" />

zh.extract:
赤胸杜鵑（學名：Cuculus solitarius）是杜鵑科杜鵑屬的其中一種。這種中型鳥類棲息於撒哈拉以南的非洲地區。在阿非利卡語中，因其鳴叫聲而被稱為「Piet-my-vrou」。


描述
赤胸杜鵑是一種中型杜鵑，體長約31公分。雄鳥背部呈石板灰色，喉部與頭側為淺灰色，尾羽為深灰色並帶白色尾尖。胸腹呈赤褐色或肉桂色，常帶橫紋，腹部為乳白色或淺黃褐色。雌鳥羽色相似，但胸腹色澤較暗淡，橫紋分布亦不固定。


分布和棲息地
該物種分佈於安哥拉、貝寧、波札那、布隆迪、喀麥隆、中非共和國、查德、剛果共和國、剛果民主共和國、象牙海岸、赤道幾內亞、史瓦帝尼、衣索比亞、加彭、甘比亞、迦納、幾內亞、幾內亞比紹、肯亞、萊索托、利比里亞、馬拉威、馬利、莫三比克、納米比亞、奈及利亞、盧安達、塞內加爾、塞拉利昂、索馬利亞、南非、蘇丹、坦尚尼亞、多哥、烏干達、尚比亞及辛巴威。在南非地區，赤胸杜鵑是常見的繁殖遷徙鳥類，分布範圍遍及該區域，僅乾燥的西部地區除外。赤胸杜鵑偏好的棲息地為林地。通常可見其獨居活動，而非與同類群聚。


行為
赤胸杜鵑通常獨居且叫聲極為頻繁，棲息於森林與種植園中。食性廣泛，包括毛毛蟲、蜘蛛、蜈蚣、千足蟲、蛞蝓、蝸牛、小型脊椎動物及漿果。
赤胸杜鵑會與多於一隻的伴侶交配（屬於一夫多妻制）。如同其他杜鵑類，赤胸杜鵑會將卵產於其他鳥類的巢中，讓受寄生的鳥類撫育杜鵑雛鳥——這些鳥類會照料雛鳥，誤以為是自己的後代（巢寄生）。約有十五種小型鳥類成為受害者，其中最常見的寄主包括：黄喉歌䳭（Cossypha caffra）、海角鶺鴒（Motacilla capensis）以及白眉歌䳭（Cossypha humeralis）。寄主家庭隨後撫養這隻雛鳥。該鳥產下的蛋呈褐色，每季在不同巢穴中產卵數量約為20顆。


參考資料


外部連結
Red-chested cuckoo—Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds
